critique.summary: The article introduces purl, a command-line interface tool similar to curl, designed to make HTTP requests that require payment. Developed by Stripe, purl allows users to interact with paid endpoints by integrating payment functionality directly into HTTP requests.
mainPoints: purl is a CLI tool inspired by curl for making HTTP requests that require payment.
It is built by Stripe and the source code is available on GitHub.
Users can install purl via Homebrew or a shell script.
purl supports wallet setup and can handle both free and paid HTTP endpoints, with an example of a paid endpoint costing 0.01 USDC.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about purl being a curl-like CLI tool for paid HTTP requests built by Stripe are accurate based on the official purl.dev website and Stripe's announcements. The installation methods and usage examples correspond with the documentation provided by Stripe. There is no indication of misinformation or exaggeration in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention potential security or privacy implications of using a payment-enabled HTTP client.
There is no discussion of supported payment methods beyond USDC or how payments are processed and verified.
The article lacks information on the scope of use cases or limitations of purl in real-world applications.
No mention is made of compatibility with existing curl scripts or integration with other tools.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new CLI tool 'purl' designed for making paid HTTP requests, with users questioning its practical adoption, security concerns, and potential latency issues. There is also commentary on naming collisions and documentation quality.

comments.author: acheong08
comments.text:
A couple questions:

- The default seems to make the payment without confirmation. What stops an endpoint from changing payment amount between an inspect request and the actual request?

- Will adoption of this payment protocol ever grow large enough for anyone to implement this on either the client or server?

- Bots have more of a financial incentive to crawl sites than a human. I doubt this will actually stop anything

- I see a AGENTS.md. How much of this is vibe coded? It's near impossible to get a sense of the care taken to review LLM output. Hard to trust with money.
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about security, payment confirmation, and adoption challenges, which are critical for a payment-based HTTP request system.

comments.author: rbtms
comments.text:
While I can understand the rationale behind it, I can't imagine this being used for anything that requires low latency requests.

I imagine the flow would be something like (correct me if I'm wrong) [client request] -> [backend returns an error]/[accepts the request and waits for payment] -> [client sends the money] -> [backend accepts the transaction] -> [backend returns the requested data]. All of this sounds like a huge bloat over the current API key/token system.
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies potential latency and complexity issues inherent in a payment-required HTTP request flow compared to traditional API authentication.

comments.author: connorgurney
comments.text: It only took us 29 years but HTTP 402 Payment Required might finally mean something on a wider scale…
comments.analysis: Makes a clever observation about the rarely used HTTP 402 status code gaining relevance, reflecting an insightful historical perspective.

comments.author: kiallmacinnes
comments.text:
An annoying trend I've been seeing recently, which the GitHub repo behind this does, is having better documentation for the robots than there is for the users.

Compare the README.md to the skills/pay-for-http-request/SKILL.md
comments.analysis: Highlights a common documentation issue that can hinder user adoption and understanding, a practical critique of the project's communication.

comments.author: rtpg
comments.text:
Uncharacteristically unclear marketing from Stripe!

You're gonna have to give me more to go off of than this.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration with the marketing clarity, which is a subjective but relevant point about communication effectiveness.

comments.author: throwaway-538
comments.text:
Oh no, this is already the second "purl" Name collision...

https://github.com/package-url/purl-spec

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persistent_uniform_resource_lo...

These things are always a massive source of confusion...
comments.analysis: Correctly points out naming collisions that can confuse users, a valid concern in software naming conventions.

comments.author: tejtm
comments.text:
purl: persistent uniform resource locator  (at least since 1995)
[] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persistent_uniform_resource_lo...
comments.analysis: Provides historical context on the acronym 'purl' but adds little to the discussion about the new tool itself.

comments.author: gforce_de
comments.text:
user@NAS:~$ curl -fsSl https://www.purl.dev/install.sh | bash
  ...
  purl: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.38' not found (required by purl)
  
  user@NAS:~$ uname -a
  Linux NAS 6.1.0-43-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.162-1 (2026-02-08) x86_64 GNU/Linux
comments.analysis: Reports a technical issue related to system compatibility, useful for troubleshooting but limited in broader discussion.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text: what are the use cases here?
comments.analysis: Asks a fundamental question about practical applications, encouraging clarification but is brief and lacks detail.
